lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The grey-eyed morn smiles on the frowning night,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Chequering the eastern clouds with streaks of light,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And flecked darkness like a drunkard reels

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: From forth day’s path and Titan’s fiery wheels:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Now, ere the sun advance his burning eye,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The day to cheer and night’s dank dew to dry,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I must up-fill this osier cage of ours

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: With baleful weeds and precious-juiced flowers.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The earth that’s nature’s mother is her tomb;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What is her burying grave that is her womb,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And from her womb children of divers kind

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: We sucking on her natural bosom find,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Many for many virtues excellent,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: None but for some and yet all different.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O, mickle is the powerful grace that lies

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In herbs, plants, stones, and their true qualities:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For nought so vile that on the earth doth live

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But to the earth some special good doth give,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Nor aught so good but strain’d from that fair use

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Revolts from true birth, stumbling on abuse:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And vice sometimes by action dignified.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Within the infant rind of this small flower

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Poison hath residence and medicine power:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For this, being smelt, with that part cheers each part;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Being tasted, slays all senses with the heart.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Two such opposed kings encamp them still

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In man as well as herbs, grace and rude will;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And where the worser is predominant,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Full soon the canker death eats up that plant.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Good morrow, father.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Benedicite!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What early tongue so sweet saluteth me?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Young son, it argues a distemper’d head

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: So soon to bid good morrow to thy bed:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Care keeps his watch in every old man’s eye,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And where care lodges, sleep will never lie;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But where unbruised youth with unstuff’d brain

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Doth couch his limbs, there golden sleep doth reign:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Therefore thy earliness doth me assure

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou art up-roused by some distemperature;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Or if not so, then here I hit it right,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Our Romeo hath not been in bed to-night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That last is true; the sweeter rest was mine.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: God pardon sin! wast thou with Rosaline?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With Rosaline, my ghostly father? no;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I have forgot that name, and that name’s woe.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: That’s my good son: but where hast thou been, then?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I’ll tell thee, ere thou ask it me again.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I have been feasting with mine enemy,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Where on a sudden one hath wounded me,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That’s by me wounded: both our remedies

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Within thy help and holy physic lies:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I bear no hatred, blessed man, for, lo,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My intercession likewise steads my foe.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Riddling confession finds but riddling shrift.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Then plainly know my heart’s dear love is set

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: On the fair daughter of rich Capulet:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As mine on hers, so hers is set on mine;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And all combined, save what thou must combine

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: By holy marriage: when and where and how

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: We met, we woo’d and made exchange of vow,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I’ll tell thee as we pass; but this I pray,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That thou consent to marry us to-day.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Holy Saint Francis, what a change is here!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is Rosaline, whom thou didst love so dear,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: So soon forsaken? young men’s love then lies

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Jesu Maria, what a deal of brine

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hath wash’d thy sallow cheeks for Rosaline!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: How much salt water thrown away in waste,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To season love, that of it doth not taste!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The sun not yet thy sighs from heaven clears,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy old groans ring yet in my ancient ears;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Lo, here upon thy cheek the stain doth sit

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Of an old tear that is not wash’d off yet:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: If e’er thou wast thyself and these woes thine,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou and these woes were all for Rosaline:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And art thou changed? pronounce this sentence then,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Women may fall, when there’s no strength in men.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou chid’st me oft for loving Rosaline.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For doting, not for loving, pupil mine.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And bad’st me bury love.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Not in a grave,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To lay one in, another out to have.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: I pray thee, chide not: she whom I love now

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth grace for grace and love for love allow;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The other did not so.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O, she knew well

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy love did read by rote and could not spell.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But come, young waverer, come, go with me,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In one respect I’ll thy assistant be;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For this alliance may so happy prove,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To turn your households’ rancour to pure love.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, let us hence; I stand on sudden haste.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Wisely and slow; they stumble that run fast.

stage_directions.text: Enter Friar Laurence, with a basket.

glosses.definition: many plants have many excellent properties

glosses.phrase: Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied
glosses.definition: the Friar's moral centerpiece — good intent misused becomes evil
the young bark or skin of the plant

glosses.phrase: Poison hath residence and medicine power
glosses.definition: the Friar's double nature: a single plant holds both cure and poison — Friar's plot will prove this

glosses.phrase: Full soon the canker death eats up that plant
glosses.definition: death soon kills even a well-ordered plant — mortality is universal
two opposing monarchs always camp in us — grace and will
unworn young man; not yet bruised by love's defeats
Jesus and Mary — Friar's exclamation of disbelief
alchemical metaphor — love refining baser feuding into gold
the Friar's warning that will go unheeded; proverb for cautious judgment
haste makes for falls — the Friar's advice and the play's epitaph

annotations.title: Friar Laurence's moral philosophy — poison and medicine in one root
annotations.body: The Friar's opening soliloquy is the play's moral centerpiece. 'Within the infant rind of this weak flower / Poison hath residence and medicine power' — a single plant contains both the remedy and the kill, and only the user's intent makes the difference. 'Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied, / And vice sometime's by action dignified.' This is Aristotelian ethics compressed into herbalism: the same substance is good or bad according to use. The philosophy matters because the Friar will test it in his own plot: the sleeping-draught he gives Juliet is intended to save her (medicine) but functionally kills both lovers (poison). The Friar is not a holy fool. He is the play's applied moral philosopher, and his plan fails because the material world (plague quarantine, missed letters, timing) does not cooperate with even the best intentions. His actions have real consequences — Shakespeare's moral universe does not excuse motive when outcome is catastrophe.
sources: Douglas Peterson, Time, Tide, and Tempest (1973)

annotations.title: 'Wisely and slow; they stumble that run fast'
annotations.body: The Friar's advice to Romeo — 'Wisely and slow; they stumble that run fast' — would if heeded stop the tragedy. It will be ignored almost immediately. This warning joins a chorus of advisory lines the play gives its lovers and audience ('these violent delights have violent ends,' 'too rash, too unadvised, too sudden'). The tragedy is not caused by the absence of wisdom; it is caused by speed overrunning it. Shakespeare repeatedly shows characters who know exactly what they should do and cannot bring themselves to do it — which is a much harder and more interesting shape of tragedy than mere ignorance.

trials.prompt: What moral claim does the Friar's herb-philosophy make?
options: A single plant holds both poison and medicine — use (intent and action) determines virtue
Only the Church's plants are safe
All plants are harmful unless blessed

trials.prompt: The Friar's 'Wisely and slow; they stumble that run fast' functions as —
options: A warning whose ignoring will drive the tragedy — the play gives its characters the advice they fail to take
